title: Share of people who have depressive disorders, by age
subtitle: Estimated share of people who have  [depressive disorders](#dod:depressive_disorders), whether or not they are diagnosed, based on representative surveys, medical data and statistical modelling.
caption:
Share of people who have depressive disorders, by age

Estimated share of people who have  depressive disorders, whether or not they are diagnosed, based on representative surveys, medical data and statistical modelling.

This horizontal bar chart displays the estimated share of people with depressive disorders across different age groups for the world in 2023. The data is presented as percentages, with bars arranged vertically by age category. The age-standardized rate stands at 3.81%, while the figure for all ages combined is 4%. The distribution shows a distinct pattern where prevalence is lowest among children and rises significantly in adulthood. The bar for ages 5-14 indicates a share of 0.98%, the lowest value on the chart. The share increases to 4.55% for ages 15-19 and continues to climb through the 20s and 30s. The highest prevalence rates appear in the older adult categories. The bar for ages 65-69 reaches 5.46%, representing the maximum value visible in this dataset. Surrounding this peak, the bars for ages 60-64, 70+, 55-59, and 50-54 all cluster closely together, ranging between 5.33% and 5.4%. Specifically, ages 60-64 sit at 5.4%, ages 70+ at 5.36%, ages 55-59 at 5.39%, and ages 50-54 at 5.33%. The middle age groups from 30 to 49 show a gradual decline from these highs, with values falling between 5% and 5.29%. The data shows that the share of people with depressive disorders is lowest in childhood, rises through young adulthood, remains elevated through middle age, and reaches its highest levels in the population aged 50 to 69.
